summary:
The WHO recommends using fiscal measures like taxes, regulating sugary product advertising, and mandating clearer labels but does NOT include routine dentist office advice about diet among its primary strategies for managing high sugar consumption. Thus, option B stands out because it represents an intervention less aligned with population-wide public health initiatives focused explicitly on curbing sugars through systemic approaches instead of individualized guidance at specific healthcare visits unrelated solely to nutrition or weight management concerns addressed here indirectly via general wellness promotion. Agent1 summarized:

The WHO suggests tax-based incentives, regulatory actions against misleading advertisements, and transparent nutritional information to combat overconsumption of added sugars. Chair-side dietary consultations during medical appointments fall short compared to more widespread policy solutions aimed directly at limiting access to excessively sweet foods and drinks. 

This summary captures the essential comparison between effective broad-scale policies versus localized patient interactions without expanding unnecessarily into additional details from other sources discussed earlier. It maintains clarity around which options meet core criteria set forth by international organizations regarding best practice recommendations related to addressing rising levels of sucrose ingestion globally.
